findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Approach's 'classified-confidence provenance' claim with the `_write_link_event` call signature shown in the Files section, and add an AC assertion that the auto-derived link carries the expected confidence/provenance field.
evidence: 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative' (Approach section)
'_write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)' — no confidence argument shown (Files / transition_close.py)

findings.finding: The Approach section claims 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' implying a confidence/provenance field is written on the link, but the Files section describes `_write_link_event` being called with no mention of a confidence argument, and no AC verifies that a confidence attribute appears on the resulting link.
findings.location: Approach section ('Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance'); Files / transition_close.py (_write_link_event call); all four ACs
scenarios: Implementer writes the link without a confidence field because the call signature shown omits it; the Approach's provenance guarantee is silently unmet.
A reviewer checks the output JSON and finds no 'confidence' attribute, contradicting the documented behaviour.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add the confidence argument to the `_write_link_event` call description (e.g., `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir, confidence='classified')`) and add an AC line like `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by").confidence=="classified"'`; or remove the provenance claim from the Approach if the field is not actually written.

The plan says 'transition.py — parse --caused-by <id>' but the full threading (adding a 5th return value to _parse_flags, a caused_by kwarg to transition_compute and close_ticket) is unspecified and unverified — a concrete parameter-threading path that must exist for the feature to work is absent from the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define how the `classified`-confidence provenance label for auto-derived `caused_by` links is persisted: either extend the LINK event payload (`{target_id, relation, confidence, derived_by}`) and update `_write_link_event`'s signature, or explicitly document that the label is NOT stored in the event and is only a human-readable claim in the docs.
evidence: Approach: 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative.'
_links.py:144-149: `_seam.append_event(source_id, 'LINK', {'target_id': target_id, 'relation': relation}, Path(tracker_dir))` — payload has no confidence/provenance field
_write_link_event signature (line 118-123) takes only source_id, target_id, relation, tracker_dir — no confidence param
metrics/registry.py:41: `CONFIDENCES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({'high', 'classified'})` — this vocabulary belongs to MetricSpec/MetricValue, not to LINK events
common.schema.json:17 defines the link relation enum but has no confidence field in the link schema
findings.finding: The Approach section declares 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' but the LINK event payload written by `_write_link_event` is confirmed to be strictly `{target_id, relation}` with no confidence/provenance field. The plan never defines a schema extension to store this label, no new parameter is added to `_write_link_event`, and the `classified` vocabulary in `metrics/registry.py` applies to MetricValue/MetricSpec — not to link events. The referenced provenance label has no defined storage location or schema binding.
findings.location: Files section (blame.py) / Approach section — 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance'
scenarios: An implementer writes the auto-link but has nowhere to store the 'classified' label — the claim is either silently dropped or requires a schema migration that the plan does not specify.
A downstream consumer trying to distinguish auto-derived from manually-asserted caused_by links cannot do so because no confidence field is written.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `metadata` dict to the LINK event payload for auto-derived links, e.g. `{'target_id': culprit_id, 'relation': 'caused_by', 'derived_by': 'blame', 'confidence': 'classified'}`, and update the `_write_link_event` call site in `transition_close.py` accordingly. Alternatively, if no storage is intended, remove the provenance claim from the Approach section to avoid an implementability gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `blame.py` invokes `git blame --porcelain` (or `-p`) so per-group SHA headers are machine-parseable, consistent with how the rest of the codebase pins git output formats (e.g. `--format=%B%x00` in `transition_close.py:43`).
evidence: Plan: 'the resolver returns a culprit commit ONLY when a strict majority (> 50%) of the blamed lines across the file_impact files point to ONE commit' — requires per-line SHA extraction from git blame output
No existing git blame invocations in src/rebar (confirmed by search)
Existing git invocations in transition_close.py always pin the format: `--format=%B%x00`
Default git blame output is locale-sensitive and not machine-parseable without --porcelain
findings.finding: The plan calls for `blame.py` to parse per-line commit SHAs from `git blame` output over `file_impact` files, but never specifies the output format flag. Default `git blame` produces human-readable annotated output that is fragile to parse (locale, config, line-count prefixes); the machine-parseable `--porcelain` (or `-p`) format is the established prior art for this use-case and is the only format that gives a guaranteed `<40-char-sha> <orig-line> <final-line> <count>` per-group header. Zero existing uses of `git blame` exist in `src/rebar`, so this is an unverified capability assertion — the plan asserts the SHA-counting algorithm works without pinning the format.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` / Approach section — 'Single-culprit threshold'
scenarios: Implementation chooses a regex against default blame output; output differs under a user's core.abbrev or color config, silently yielding wrong SHA counts and a wrong or missing caused_by link.
A binary or empty file_impact entry causes git blame to exit non-zero with no defined fallback in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` and extract the 40-char SHA from lines matching `^[0-9a-f]{40} ` (the porcelain group header). This is the established prior art for programmatic blame parsing and is locale/config-independent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git blame` output format flag (`--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain`) in `blame.py`'s spec so the per-line SHA extraction is unambiguous and yields full 40-char SHAs.
evidence: Plan: 'blame the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (`<fixing-commit>~1`) — over the bug's recorded `file_impact`'
Plan: 'the resolver returns a culprit commit ONLY when a strict majority (> 50%) of the blamed lines across the `file_impact` files point to ONE commit'
Search result: zero matches for `git blame` anywhere in `src/rebar` — this is a first-use integration surface
Search result: zero matches for `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain` outside of `git status --porcelain` usages
Evidence class: Unverified (specific `git blame` subcommand surface and required flag not confirmed in plan text)
findings.finding: `git blame` is introduced as a new external CLI surface (zero existing `git blame` invocations in `src/rebar`) but the plan never specifies the output format flag. Default `git blame` emits human-readable output with 8-character SHA prefixes, not full machine-parseable commit SHAs. Extracting per-line commit SHAs to count a dominant culprit requires `--porcelain` (full 40-char SHA per commit block, repeated for each line attributed to it) or `--line-porcelain` (a full header per line). Without the flag, the implementer must reverse-engineer the format, risking silent short-SHA collisions or broken parsing.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`, Blame target / Single-culprit threshold paragraphs
scenarios: Implementer uses default `git blame` output and parses 8-char prefixes, producing truncated SHAs that fail to match the full SHA in a commit's `rebar-ticket:` trailer lookup — culprit link is silently dropped for every bug.
Two commits share the same 8-char prefix (SHA collision at short length) — wrong commit is attributed.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --line-porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>`: each line's block starts with a full 40-char SHA, making per-line commit attribution trivially parseable with a `line.split()[0]` on header lines (those not starting with a tab).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'git blame <fixing-commit>~1' over file_impact paths will yield parseable, per-line commit SHAs, but does not specify the git blame output format. The default git blame output is human-readable prose, not machine-parseable; only --porcelain or --line-porcelain reliably yields commit SHAs per blamed line. No prior blame subprocess exists in src/rebar to establish a format convention.
findings.location: Files section: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the pre-fix state'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Uses `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each output block's first line is the 40-char commit SHA, which is parsed directly without regex over human-readable output.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `git blame` as a new external CLI surface (zero existing `git blame` calls in src/rebar) but does not specify the output format flag. The default `git blame` output is human-readable prose; only `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain` reliably yields stable, per-line commit SHA blocks machine-parseable enough to extract the culprit SHA without an ad-hoc regex. Without specifying the format, the implementer may choose the wrong format and produce a fragile parser that silently falls back to None on any moderately complex blame output (spaces in filenames, boundary commits, uncommitted-change markers), defeating the feature.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the **pre-fix** state … over the bug's recorded `file_impact`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Invokes `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each blamed hunk begins with the 40-char commit SHA on its own line, parsed directly without regex over human-readable output. Returns None if git exits non-zero (including when `<fixing-commit>~1` does not exist, e.g. a root commit).'

Tests section lists exactly three scenarios; docs-grep test is absent from that list
ABSENCE: test file `test_caused_by_autoderive.py` is described as covering the three integration scenarios only
findings.finding: AC#4 says the docs advisory is 'asserted by a test/grep,' but the Tests section describes only three integration-test scenarios (single-culprit auto-link, ambiguous no-link, --caused-by override) and makes no mention of a docs-grep or content-assertion test, leaving AC#4's verification mechanism undescribed in the implementation decomposition.
findings.location: AC#4 ('asserted by a test/grep that the section and the `--caused-by` guidance are present'); Tests section
scenarios: The docs advisory section is written but the grep/test that AC#4 requires is never implemented because it is not mentioned in the Tests decomposition, leaving AC#4 permanently unmet.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the validation policy for `--caused-by <id>`: either 'accept verbatim (no existence check, emits a warning if unresolvable)' or 'reject if the ticket does not exist in the store', so the executor doesn't need to decide.
evidence: 'parse `--caused-by <id>`' — no validation semantics stated
AC3 only tests a valid ID path; no spec for invalid/unknown IDs
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` parameter spec does not state whether the supplied ID is validated (e.g. must resolve to an existing ticket) or accepted verbatim, and what happens on an unresolvable ID at close time — this is a minor defaultable gap but an executor must choose one behavior.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — parse `--caused-by <id>`; Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: An executor who validates the ID will reject a typo-ed `--caused-by` and block the close; an executor who accepts it verbatim will silently create a dangling link — the plan does not distinguish.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one clause to the transition.py spec: 'If `--caused-by <id>` is supplied and `<id>` does not resolve to a known ticket, emit a warning and skip the link (do not block close) — consistent with the best-effort posture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and integration test for `--caused-by <nonexistent-id>`: specify and assert whether it raises a validation error or silently skips (no dangling link written), covering the failure path for this new user-facing CLI flag.
evidence: AC line 3 only asserts presence of the link on a valid `--caused-by <id>`; no AC or test case covers `--caused-by <nonexistent-id>`
Plan states 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close' for auto-derivation, but does not extend or clarify this policy for an explicitly supplied `--caused-by` value that references no known ticket
ABSENCE: no test fixture or assertion for invalid `--caused-by` input in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_caused_by_autoderive.py`
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` CLI param has no specified or tested behavior for an invalid/non-existent ticket ID — the plan covers only the happy path (ID resolves to a real ticket) with no failure-path AC or test.
findings.location: Tests section / AC line 3 (`rebar transition <bug> ... --caused-by <id>`) and `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py`
scenarios: User typos a ticket ID in `--caused-by`; a dangling link is silently written or an unhandled exception surfaces — neither outcome is specified or tested
CI pipeline passes green while the invalid-ID path is broken or produces silent garbage data
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --caused-by NONEXISTENT` either exits non-zero with a clear error message (preferred, validates ticket exists before writing) or is silently ignored (and the test asserts no `caused_by` link is written). Pick one and encode it in both the resolver and the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that when `--caused-by <id>` is supplied the auto-derive path is skipped entirely (override semantics), OR add an explicit `_is_active_link` guard in `blame.py`'s caller before invoking `_write_link_event` for the auto-derived culprit to prevent duplicate LINK events when both paths resolve the same target.
evidence: _write_link_event docstring: 'Write a single LINK event to source_id's directory (no cycle check, no idempotency)' (graph/_links.py:124)
Plan says --caused-by 'augments/override' auto-derive, but no statement that auto-derive is skipped when --caused-by is present
add_dependency has an idempotency guard (_is_active_link check at _links.py:266) that _write_link_event intentionally omits
findings.finding: When both `--caused-by <id>` is supplied AND blame auto-derive resolves the same culprit, `_write_link_event` (which has NO idempotency — 'no cycle check, no idempotency', _links.py:124) will write two identical LINK events to the bug ticket. The plan says `--caused-by` 'augments/override' but never explicitly states that auto-derivation is suppressed when `--caused-by` is present, nor that an `_is_active_link` guard is checked before the auto-derive write.
findings.location: Files section: transition_close.py paragraph — 'Same direct-writer path is used for the explicit --caused-by override applied at close time'
scenarios: User passes `--caused-by abc1` and blame also resolves to abc1 — two caused_by LINK events for the same (source, target, relation) tuple are written to the bug ticket's event log
Subsequent `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq '.links'` may emit the link once (reducer deduplication unclear) or twice, causing confusion
findings.suggested_fix: In `transition_close.py`, call `blame.resolve_culprit(...)` only when `--caused-by` was NOT supplied; when `--caused-by` is present, use it directly as the culprit (skipping blame entirely). Document this as 'explicit --caused-by suppresses auto-derivation' in the plan.

findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` parameter has no specified validation: the plan does not address what happens when the user supplies a non-existent, malformed, or self-referential ticket ID, leaving the error path unspecified for a user-facing input.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — AC line 3 / Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a configurable subprocess timeout (e.g. 10 s default) to all git calls in `blame.py`, catching `TimeoutExpired` and returning None so the close path degrades gracefully rather than hanging.
evidence: Plan relies on 'git log' and 'git blame' calls with no mention of subprocess timeout, SIGALRM, or exception handling
'Failure to resolve never blocks the close' is stated but no mechanism to guarantee it when the subprocess hangs (vs. returns an error)
findings.finding: Git subprocesses (git blame, git log) invoked by blame.py have no timeout or error-handling boundary specified; a slow or hung git call will block the close command indefinitely rather than degrading gracefully.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — 'Fixing-commit discovery' and 'Blame target' sections
scenarios: Large monorepo where git blame on a broad file_impact set takes >30 s
Git daemon unresponsive; close command hangs until terminal timeout
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap subprocess calls with `subprocess.run(..., timeout=BLAME_TIMEOUT_S)`, catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` and `subprocess.CalledProcessError`, log a warning, and return None from the resolver.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or AGENTS.md / design-doc section) documenting the `_write_link_event` direct-bypass decision: why the closed-source guard is intentionally skipped here, the safe-use preconditions, and why no interface/event boundary was introduced.
evidence: 'writes via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir) directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc section is mentioned anywhere in the plan for this decision.
T5e(c): 'a novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The `_write_link_event` direct bypass of `graph._links` private API is a novel architectural decision that intentionally circumvents the system's link-integrity guards; no ADR or AGENTS.md update is planned to capture this decision, its rationale, and its safe-use conditions.
findings.location: Files section / `transition_close.py` description; Approach section — 'best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks close, and the edge is written via `_write_link_event` directly'
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new guard to `_links` (e.g., a referential-integrity check) and does not know `transition_close.py` bypasses all guards via the private writer — they may not protect the invariant in the bypass path.
The safe-use conditions for `_write_link_event` (only on closed-source, non-cycle-forming, non-blocking relations) are undocumented, so the next caller may reuse the pattern unsafely.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-caused-by-direct-link-write.md` (or an AGENTS.md entry) capturing: (1) context — closed-source guard blocks legitimate post-close provenance links; (2) decision — call `_write_link_event` directly for `caused_by` on closed bugs; (3) preconditions for safe reuse — source is already closed, relation is non-blocking, cycle-freedom is asserted by blame logic; (4) consequences — future guard additions must also be applied to this bypass path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at minimum a `logger.debug`/`logger.info` emission for each early-return branch in `blame.py` (no fixing commit, no majority culprit, write succeeded, write failed) so blame resolution outcomes are observable.
evidence: Plan says 'Returns None (no link) on ambiguity' but specifies no log emission on any code path
No mention of debug/info log lines, metrics counter, or trace span anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: There is no observability (logging, metric, or trace) specified for any outcome of the auto-derivation path: success (link written), skip (no fixing commit, ambiguous blame), or failure (git error, write error). Operators cannot diagnose why a `caused_by` link is absent.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — Approach section
scenarios: Bug closed, no caused_by link appears; developer cannot tell if blame was ambiguous, git failed, file_impact was empty, or trailer was absent
Production incident post-mortem: provenance link is missing but no audit trail explains why

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before calling `_write_link_event`, check whether a `caused_by` link to the same target already exists in the store and skip the write if so, making the operation idempotent under at-least-once delivery.
evidence: 'the auto-link therefore writes via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir) directly' — no mention of pre-write existence check
No idempotency guard (e.g. 'skip if caused_by link to this target already exists') is described for either the auto or explicit path
findings.finding: The direct `_write_link_event` call is not idempotent under retry: if the close command fails after writing the link event but before returning success (e.g. I/O error on the STATUS event, wrapper retry), a second invocation writes a duplicate `caused_by` link event with no deduplication check.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — 'CRITICAL ordering/guard' section; also applies to `--caused-by` path
scenarios: Network partition after _write_link_event but before close command exits; caller retries the full transition close command
CI pipeline retries a failed close step

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` as an affected caller and specify whether auto-derivation is suppressed on the import code path (e.g., via a `caused_by=None` default that skips the blame step when not explicitly provided).
evidence: src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:356: `transition_compute(local, 'open', 'closed', force=True, repo_root=rr)` — called in a bulk import loop with `force=True`
The plan states auto-derivation runs 'after `txn.transition_core` has committed the STATUS event' inside `transition_close.py`, which is in the call path of every `transition_compute` close

Import may be run against a git repo that has no commits referencing synthetic ticket IDs, making blame lookup always fail — but the plan guarantees 'failure to resolve never blocks the close', so this is an unenumerated consumer rather than a correctness bug
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` calls `transition_compute` directly (lines 338, 356) outside `_commands/` and is not enumerated; if auto-derivation runs on every close inside `transition_close.close_ticket`, the import path will attempt git-blame operations on synthetic/temp repos during NDJSON import.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` absent from edit targets
scenarios: NDJSON import of 500 closed bugs spawns 500 `git log` subprocesses looking for fixing commits — significant performance regression in bulk import.
Import of bugs with `file_impact` in a fresh repo (no fixing commits) silently skips blame but adds latency from the git subprocess.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `file_impact` field structure (e.g. `list[str]` of repo-relative file paths, or `list[{path, lines}]`) in the `blame.py` spec so the executor can implement the git-blame iteration without guessing.
evidence: 'blame the pre-fix state … over the bug's recorded `file_impact`'
'Returns None (no link) on ambiguity: no `file_impact`'
No definition of `file_impact` field shape appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `file_impact` field — which the blame resolver reads to know which files to `git blame` — is referenced throughout but never defined: its type, structure (list of file paths? file+line ranges?), and where it is recorded on a bug ticket are unspecified. An executor cannot implement `blame.py` without knowing what to iterate over.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — Blame target / 'Single-culprit' threshold sections; also Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Executor must guess whether file_impact is a list of path strings, a dict of path→line-ranges, or something else, leading to mismatched blame invocations and silent wrong results.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the blame.py section, e.g.: '`file_impact` is the list of repo-relative file paths stored on the bug ticket (type: `list[str]`); the resolver runs `git blame <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` for each path and aggregates line counts across all files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or augment AC2) that explicitly asserts the `rebar transition … closed` command exits 0 and the bug reaches `closed` status even when blame resolution fails (e.g. ambiguous blame and/or missing `file_impact`).
evidence: 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close'
AC2: 'an ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto `caused_by` link — integration test asserts absence' — asserts link absence only, not close success
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section states 'best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks close' but no acceptance criterion verifies that the close command itself succeeds when blame resolution fails (e.g. no fixing commit found, no `file_impact`). AC2 only asserts absence of the auto-link, not that the close exits 0 in the ambiguous-blame fixture.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Approach section 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close'
scenarios: If an exception in the blame resolver propagates and aborts the close, AC2's absence assertion still passes (no link was written) while the close silently failed — the claim is untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'an ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto `caused_by` link AND the close command exits 0 with the bug status == closed — integration test asserts both absence of the link and successful close.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/correction mechanism (e.g., reference an existing `rebar unlink` command, or note that `rebar link … caused_by` can overwrite the auto-derived edge) and state it in the plan so users know how to correct a false-positive auto-derived `caused_by` link.
evidence: 'the auto-link therefore writes via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir) directly' (Files / transition_close.py)
'Best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks close' (Approach) — acknowledges failure mode but not a false-positive/spurious-link scenario
ABSENCE: no plan section describes how a user removes or corrects a spuriously auto-derived caused_by link
findings.finding: The plan describes writing a `caused_by` link via `_write_link_event` directly to the store as an irreversible operation (no event-deletion API is mentioned anywhere), yet provides no rollback path or undo mechanism for a spuriously auto-derived link — this is an unacknowledged irreversibility for an automated write path.
findings.location: Files / transition_close.py ('writes via graph._links._write_link_event … directly'); Approach ('Best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks close')
scenarios: Blame resolves to a high-churn refactor commit whose ticket happens to carry a `rebar-ticket:` trailer; a false-positive `caused_by` link is written and there is no described `rebar unlink` or equivalent command to remove it.
A team audits provenance records and finds a spurious link they cannot remove, polluting the causal graph.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach (and optionally to the docs advisory) such as: 'A spuriously auto-derived link can be removed with `rebar unlink <bug> caused_by <ticket>` or corrected by re-running `rebar link <bug> caused_by <correct-ticket>`.  If no unlink command exists, note that gap and scope it as follow-on work.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the `>50%` majority threshold to a named constant (or config key, e.g. `blame_majority_threshold`) in a settings/constants module so it is configurable without touching the resolver logic.
evidence: 'the resolver returns a culprit commit ONLY when a strict majority (> 50%) of the blamed lines … point to ONE commit'
No mention of a config option, a constant extracted to a settings module, or a future measurement plan to validate the threshold.
T5e(b): 'rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded'
findings.finding: The >50% single-culprit majority threshold is hardcoded in `blame.py` with no configuration path; this heuristic is asserted rather than derived from data, and experience with real repos may call for tuning it.
findings.location: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — 'strict majority (> 50%) of the blamed lines … point to ONE commit'
scenarios: A project with many small, cosmetic single-commit reformats that touch 51% of a file's lines produces spurious `caused_by` links — the only fix requires a code change.
A team running rebar on a monorepo finds 50% too aggressive and wants 70%; they must fork the threshold constant buried in `blame.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: In `blame.py`, define `BLAME_MAJORITY_THRESHOLD: float = 0.50` (or read from rebar config) and use it in the comparison; document the default and override mechanism in the user-guide advisory section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--caused-by <id>` validates the target ticket exists in the store and is not the source bug itself before writing, emitting a clear CLI error on failure rather than bypassing all guards silently.
evidence: 'bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards — which add nothing for a non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation on a closed source' — the justification covers auto-derivation but the same bypass is applied to user-supplied --caused-by, which can reference arbitrary IDs
No AC or plan text specifies validation of the --caused-by target ticket's existence or a self-reference check
findings.finding: The plan explicitly states that `--caused-by <id>` uses the same direct-writer path that bypasses cycle guards, so a user who specifies `--caused-by <bug-id>` where the target eventually chains back to the source can silently create a provenance cycle; additionally there is no validation that the referenced ID exists in the store.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — 'Same direct-writer path is used for the explicit --caused-by override'; AC line 3
scenarios: User accidentally types `--caused-by <same-bug-id>` (self-loop)
User specifies --caused-by <id> where id does not exist in the store, silently writing a dangling reference

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact insertion point of the auto-link write within `close_ticket`'s post-close sequence — place it BEFORE `push.push_after_commit` (so the trailing push covers it) and AFTER `_compact_on_close` (so the LINK is a discrete post-compact event, symmetric with the force-close comment's placement).
evidence: Existing close_ticket sequence: transition_core (line 436) → sign attestation (line 453) → force-close comment (line 496) → _compact_on_close (line 514) → scratch.cleanup (line 515) → push.push_after_commit (line 523)
_write_link_event calls append_event which 'commits + pushes IN-PROCESS via rebar._store.event_append.write_and_push' (_seam.py docstring)
Plan says only 'the auto-link runs after txn.transition_core has committed the STATUS event' — leaves the rest of the sequence unspecified
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where in `close_ticket`'s existing post-close sequence the auto-link write is inserted. `_write_link_event` routes through `append_event` → `write_and_push`, which commits AND pushes the LINK event inline. If this call is placed after `push.push_after_commit` (line 523 in transition_close.py), the LINK commit lands after the push has already run, leaving the LINK event unpushed until the next write. If placed before `_compact_on_close`, the LINK may get folded into the SNAPSHOT by `--threshold=0` compaction.
findings.location: Files section: transition_close.py — 'the auto-link runs after txn.transition_core has committed the STATUS event'
scenarios: Auto-link write placed after push.push_after_commit: LINK event committed to local branch, not pushed; stranded until next operation triggers a push
Auto-link write placed before _compact_on_close: compact folds the LINK into the SNAPSHOT, which is harmless but may surprise readers who expect LINK events as separate files
findings.suggested_fix: Insert the blame auto-link and --caused-by link write immediately after the force-close audit comment block (after line 511) and before `_compact_on_close` (line 514), relying on `push.push_after_commit` at line 523 to push it. Document this ordering explicitly in the plan.

findings.finding: The `docs/user-guide.md` bug-template blame-hunt advisory is listed as a required deliverable in the Files section but no Acceptance Criterion covers it. The union of ACs (test pass, link present/absent, --caused-by) does not verify this file was written or is correct.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/user-guide.md`; Acceptance Criteria section (all three ACs are test-only)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `grep -q 'caused-by' docs/user-guide.md && grep -q 'blame' docs/user-guide.md` or a stronger structural check against a required heading.

findings.finding: The plan never specifies how `blame.py` identifies the *fixing commit* (`<fixing-commit>` in `<fixing-commit>~1`). The close path's existing `_referencing_commit_exists` scans the full git log for *any* commit referencing the ticket and returns True/False, not a single commit SHA. Without a specified mechanism to resolve the fixing commit, the plan's core blame logic — "the parent of the fixing commit" — has an undefined input.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`; plan phrase 'the parent of the fixing commit (`<fixing-commit>~1`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `blame.py` spec: 'Identify the fixing commit by scanning `git log --format=%H%x00%B` for the most recent commit whose message yields the bug's ticket id via `extract_ticket_refs` + `resolve_ticket_id`. If zero or ≥2 such commits are found, return None (ambiguous fixing commit).' This is consistent with the plan's existing conservative/abstain-on-ambiguity stance.

findings.finding: The described deliverable `docs/user-guide.md` (bug-template blame-hunt advisory) has no covering acceptance criterion — there is no AC that verifies the doc was added, its content is correct, or that it references the auto-derivation behavior.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/user-guide.md`; Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `docs/user-guide.md` contains a blame-hunt advisory section referencing `caused_by` auto-derivation and `--caused-by` override (verified by grep or doc-review).'

findings.finding: The plan reference `_links.py:242-247` for the closed-source guard is off by ~10 lines: the actual `source_status == 'closed'` raise is at lines 252-257. Minor, but if an implementer uses line numbers to locate the guard they will land in the cycle-check block instead.
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — CRITICAL ordering/guard note citing '_links.py:242-247'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `_links.py:242-247` with `_links.py:252-257` in the CRITICAL ordering/guard note.

findings.finding: The plan references a 'dominant culprit commit' and 'multi-commit spread' ambiguity threshold but never defines what makes a blame result 'single-culprit': is it 100% of blamed lines pointing to one commit, a majority, or something else? An executor must invent this threshold, which is a real design decision.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` description; Approach section: 'links only on a single dominant culprit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rule such as: 'single-culprit = ALL blamed lines in `file_impact` resolve to the same commit SHA; any divergence (≥2 distinct commits) is treated as multi-commit spread and returns None.'

findings.finding: The plan cites `_links.py:242-247` as the location where `add_dependency` raises `ValueError` for a closed-source ticket. Reading the file confirms the closed-source guard is actually at lines 252-257; lines 242-247 are the `check_cycle_at_level` `CyclicDependencyError` block for self-referential dependencies. The cited line range is incorrect.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — '`_links.py:242-247` raises ValueError'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`_links.py:242-247` raises ValueError' to '`_links.py:252-257` raises ValueError' in the Files section description for `transition_close.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define how `blame.py` behaves (error surface, capability flag, or documented limitation) when the git repository is not accessible from the harness process — covering the remote MCP / server-outside-checkout shape so auto-derivation failure is distinguishable from genuine ambiguity.
evidence: Plan: 'the <fixing-commit> is the most recent commit in git log whose message resolves to THIS bug ticket'
Plan: 'blame the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fixing-commit>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact'
Plan: 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close' (swallows all git errors silently)
Support matrix cell: harness=remote MCP server; project location=server outside the checkout
findings.finding: The plan's `blame.py` resolves culprit commits by shelling out to `git log` and `git blame` against the target project's working tree. This assumes the git binary is on PATH and the repository filesystem is directly accessible from the process running the harness. In a remote MCP server deployment where rebar runs on a separate server that does not have the target project's checkout mounted, both `git log` and `git blame` will fail with 'not a git repository' or 'git: command not found'. The plan's 'failure to resolve never blocks close' silently swallows all such failures, making the entire auto-derivation feature a permanent no-op for this shape with no diagnostic or fallback — users get no `caused_by` links and no error explaining why.
findings.location: Files › `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — Fixing-commit discovery and Blame target sections
scenarios: Remote MCP server harness running against a Next.js or Ruby project whose git checkout is on a developer workstation or CI runner: every `git log`/`git blame` subprocess call exits non-zero; `blame.py` returns None for every bug; no `caused_by` links are ever auto-derived; no error surface tells the user why; `--caused-by` still works but the headline auto-derivation feature is silently dead.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a capability-check at module load or first call: attempt `git rev-parse --git-dir` in the configured workspace path; if it fails, log a structured warning (e.g. `logger.warning('git-blame auto-derivation unavailable: %s', err)`) and return None — making the shape-based limitation visible rather than silently swallowed. Document in `docs/user-guide.md` that auto-derivation requires a local git checkout accessible to the harness process.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `blame.py` has ≥3 planned call-sites justifying a standalone module, or inline the single-culprit resolver as a private helper in `transition_close.py` (alongside the existing `_referencing_commit_exists` git-log walk).
evidence: The plan names one consumer of blame.py: transition_close.py's auto-link step.
No other planned or existing call-sites appear in the codebase (grep for 'blame' in src/rebar returns no relevant hits).

Rule-of-Three: a standalone module is premature without ≥3 call-sites; with one call-site, the culprit-resolver logic (fixing-commit discovery + >50% threshold) can live as a private helper inside transition_close.py itself, where the only existing comparable git-log walk already lives (_referencing_commit_exists at transition_close.py:32).
The metrics/ package (registry.py, snapshot.py) is for declarative metric specs and NDJSON time-series — a git-blame culprit resolver is architecturally misplaced there.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` is a new standalone module with exactly one planned call-site (transition_close.py), violating the Rule-of-Three for premature abstraction.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — new: single-culprit resolver
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer finds a git-blame culprit resolver in `metrics/` with one external caller — unclear why it isn't co-located with its only consumer or at least in a `_git_utils.py`.
If a second blame use-case never materializes, the module is dead weight with no abstraction benefit.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the fixing-commit discovery + single-culprit threshold logic into a private function `_resolve_culprit_commit(bug_id, tracker, repo_root) -> str | None` in `transition_close.py`, co-located with `_referencing_commit_exists`. If a second call-site emerges later, extract to a shared module at that point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of calling the private `graph._links._write_link_event` directly, expose a narrow public function (e.g. `write_caused_by_link` or `write_link_unchecked`) from `graph._links` or `graph/__init__.py` that documents the bypass contract explicitly, so the external call is against a declared surface.
evidence: Grep confirms _write_link_event has exactly 2 call-sites today, both internal to _links.py itself (lines 272 and 277).
The plan adds a third call-site from transition_close.py — the first external (cross-module) call to this private function.
Python convention: a leading underscore signals 'internal to this module'; reaching into it from a sibling module creates an implicit bypass contract with no public API declaration.

The plan's stated reason (the closed-source guard in add_dependency blocks this use) is valid, but the same bypass could be expressed as a narrow public function, e.g. `write_link_direct(source, target, relation, tracker)` exported from graph._links or graph/__init__.py.
findings.finding: The plan calls the private underscore symbol `graph._links._write_link_event` from `transition_close.py` with a single external call-site, coupling a sibling module to an internal implementation detail rather than a declared public seam.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — 'the auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` **directly**'
scenarios: If `_write_link_event`'s signature or semantics change, transition_close.py silently breaks with no public-API contract violation to flag it.
Future contributors seeing `_write_link_event` called externally may replicate the pattern elsewhere, eroding the closed-source guard entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a public `write_link_unchecked(source_id, target_id, relation, tracker_dir)` to `graph/_links.py` (or re-export from `graph/__init__.py`) with a docstring noting 'bypasses closed-source and cycle guards — for use only when source is already closed and the relation is non-blocking'. Have transition_close.py call this public name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate widening `add_dependency`'s `source_status == 'closed'` guard to permit non-blocking/directional relations (the same set already exempted from cycle detection at `_graph.py:204`) as the prior-art in-seam fix, rather than bypassing via private `_write_link_event`; document why the bypass is preferred if the in-seam fix is rejected.
evidence: Plan: 'the auto-link therefore writes via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir) directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
_graph.py:204: 'if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):' — already exempted from cycle detection via a named relation set
_links.py:252-257: source_status == 'closed' raise applies uniformly to all relations including the non-blocking ones
No prior-art consideration or ADR reference in the plan for the bypass choice
findings.finding: The plan chooses to bypass `add_dependency`'s `source_status == 'closed'` guard by calling the private `_write_link_event` directly, but does not consider the established alternative of relaxing the guard inside `add_dependency` for non-blocking, directional relations. The codebase already applies this exact pattern for cycle guards: `_graph.py:204` exempts `relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from / caused_by` from cycle detection. Symmetrically widening `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard for the same set of relations would be the prior-art in-seam fix — safer than an out-of-seam private-API bypass that every future caller must independently replicate.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` / Approach section — 'Best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks close, and the edge is written via `_write_link_event` directly so the closed-source guard does not silently swallow it'
scenarios: A future caller wants to add a `discovered_from` or `supersedes` link from a closed source and must also discover and bypass the same guard, spreading the private-API bypass pattern.

An implementer reviews `add_dependency`'s contract, sees 'Raises ValueError if source is closed', and adds their own bypass without realising the guard was widened elsewhere — two inconsistent bypass paths.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
